content:
# Genesis Chapter 16 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 9r-9v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_016_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_016_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 11-12 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0015

---

*Sarai's Plan: Hagar Given to Abram (vv. 1–3)*

**16:1** And Sarai [וְשָׂרַי] wife of Abram [אֵשֶׁת אַבְרָם] had borne him no child [לֹא יָלְדָה לוֹ] and she had [וְלָהּ] a female slave [שִׁפְחָה] Egyptian [מִצְרִית] and her name [וּשְׁמָהּ] was Hagar [הָגָר].

**16:2** And Sarai said [וַתֹּאמֶר שָׂרַי] to Abram [אֶל-אַבְרָם]: Look now [הִנֵּה-נָא] — the LORD has restrained me [עֲצָרַנִי יְהוָה] from bearing [מִלֶּדֶת] — come in now [בֹּא-נָא] to my slave [אֶל-שִׁפְחָתִי] perhaps I shall be built up [אִוָּלֵד] through her [מִמֶּנָּה]. And Abram listened to [וַיִּשְׁמַע אַבְרָם] the voice of Sarai [לְקוֹל שָׂרָי].

**16:3** And Sarai [וַתִּקַּח שָׂרַי] wife of Abram [אֵשֶׁת אַבְרָם] took [וַתִּקַּח] Hagar the Egyptian [אֶת-הָגָר הַמִּצְרִית] her slave [שִׁפְחָתָהּ] at the end of ten years [מִקֵּץ עֶשֶׂר שָׁנִים] of Abram's dwelling [לְשֶׁבֶת אַבְרָם] in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] and she gave her [וַתִּתֵּן אֹתָהּ] to Abram her husband [לְאַבְרָם אִישָׁהּ] as a wife for him [לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה].

---

*Hagar Conceives — Conflict Between the Women (vv. 4–6)*

**16:4** And he came in [וַיָּבֹא] to Hagar [אֶל-הָגָר] and she conceived [וַתַּהַר] and she saw [וַתֵּרֶא] that she had conceived [כִּי הָרָתָה] and her mistress became slight [וַתֵּקַל גְּבִרְתָּהּ] in her eyes [בְּעֵינֶיהָ].

**16:5** And Sarai said [וַתֹּאמֶר שָׂרַי] to Abram [אֶל-אַבְרָם]: My wrong be upon you [חֲמָסִי עָלֶיךָ] — I myself [אָנֹכִי] gave my slave [נָתַתִּי שִׁפְחָתִי] into your bosom [בְּחֵיקֶךָ] and she saw [וַתֵּרֶא] that she had conceived [כִּי הָרָתָה] and I became slight [וָאֵקַל] in her eyes [בְּעֵינֶיהָ] — may the LORD judge [יִשְׁפֹּט יְהוָה] between me and between you [בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶיךָ].

**16:6** And Abram said [וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָם] to Sarai [אֶל-שָׂרַי]: Look [הִנֵּה] — your slave is in your hand [שִׁפְחָתֵךְ בְּיָדֵךְ] — do to her [עֲשִׂי-לָהּ] the good in your eyes [הַטּוֹב בְּעֵינָיִךְ]. And Sarai afflicted her [וַתְּעַנֶּהָ שָׂרַי] and she fled [וַתִּבְרַח] from her face [מִפָּנֶיהָ].

---

*The Angel of the LORD Finds Hagar at the Spring (vv. 7–12)*

**16:7** And the angel of the LORD [וַיִּמְצָאָהּ מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה] found her [וַיִּמְצָאָהּ] at the spring of water [עַל-עֵין הַמַּיִם] in the wilderness [בַּמִּדְבָּר] — at the spring [עַל-הָעַיִן] on the road [עַל-הַדֶּרֶךְ] to Shur [שׁוּר].

**16:8** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Hagar [הָגָר] slave of Sarai [שִׁפְחַת שָׂרַי] from where have you come [מֵאַיִן בָּאת] and where are you going [וְאָנָה תֵלֵכִי]? And she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: From the face of Sarai my mistress [מִפְּנֵי שָׂרַי גְּבִרְתִּי] I am fleeing [אָנֹכִי בֹּרַחַת].

**16:9** And the angel of the LORD said to her [וַיֹּאמֶר לָהּ מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה]: Return [שׁוּבִי] to your mistress [אֶל-גְּבִרְתֵּךְ] and afflict yourself [וְהִתְעַנִּי] under her hands [תַּחַת יָדֶיהָ].

**16:10** And the angel of the LORD said to her [וַיֹּאמֶר לָהּ מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה]: Multiplying I will multiply [הַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה] your seed [אֶת-זַרְעֵךְ] and it will not be counted [וְלֹא יִסָּפֵר] for multitude [מֵרֹב].

**16:11** And the angel of the LORD said to her [וַיֹּאמֶר לָהּ מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה]: Look [הִנָּךְ] — you are with child [הָרָה] and you will bear a son [וְיָלַדְתְּ בֵּן] and you shall call his name [וְקָרָאת שְׁמוֹ] Ishmael [יִשְׁמָעֵאל] — for the LORD has heard [כִּי-שָׁמַע יְהוָה] your affliction [אֶל-עָנְיֵךְ].

**16:12** And he will be a wild-donkey of a man [וְהוּא יִהְיֶה פֶּרֶא אָדָם] — his hand against everyone [יָדוֹ בַכֹּל] and everyone's hand against him [וְיַד כֹּל בּוֹ] and before the face of all his brothers [וְעַל-פְּנֵי כָל-אֶחָיו] he will dwell [יִשְׁכֹּן].

---

*Hagar Names God: El Roi (vv. 13–14)*

**16:13** And she called the name of the LORD [וַתִּקְרָא שֵׁם-יְהוָה] who was speaking to her [הַדֹּבֵר אֵלֶיהָ]: You are El Roi [אַתָּה אֵל רֳאִי] — for she said [כִּי אָמְרָה] — have I really seen here [הֲגַם הֲלֹם רָאִיתִי] after the one who sees me [אַחֲרֵי רֹאִי]?

**16:14** Therefore [עַל-כֵּן] the well was called [קָרָא לַבְּאֵר] Beer-lahai-roi [בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי] — look [הִנֵּה] it is between Kadesh and between Bered [בֵּין-קָדֵשׁ וּבֵין בָּרֶד].

---

*Ishmael Born (vv. 15–16)*

**16:15** And Hagar bore [וַתֵּלֶד הָגָר] to Abram [לְאַבְרָם] a son [בֵּן] and Abram called [וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָם] the name of his son [שֶׁם-בְּנוֹ] whom Hagar bore [אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה הָגָר] Ishmael [יִשְׁמָעֵאל].

**16:16** And Abram [וְאַבְרָם] was the son of eighty-six years [בֶּן-שְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה וְשֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים] when Hagar bore [בְּלֶדֶת-הָגָר] Ishmael to Abram [אֶת-יִשְׁמָעֵאל לְאַבְרָם].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 16:1 — שִׁפְחָה מִצְרִית: The Egyptian Slave-Woman

**שִׁפְחָה מִצְרִית** — "a female slave, Egyptian."

The noun **שִׁפְחָה** (shifchah) designates a female slave of the lowest social category, as distinct from **אָמָה** (amah), which can denote a female slave with certain household rights or a concubine-slave with more elevated legal status. The word order in Hebrew — common noun first, then gentilic — is standard; the gentilic **מִצְרִית** (mitztrit, "Egyptian") is significant. Egypt is where Abram and Sarai had sojourned in 12:10-20; the plausible implication is that Hagar was acquired during or after that episode, perhaps as part of the wealth Pharaoh gave Abram (12:16: "he had sheep and cattle and donkeys and male servants and female servants"). If so, Hagar is a direct consequence of the Egyptian sojourn — Sarai's near-loss in Egypt is repaid by the Hagar crisis at home.

The name **הָגָר** (Hagar) is possibly connected to the root **הָגַר** (hagar, "to sojourn") or to an Egyptian word — though its etymology in Hebrew is uncertain. The name will recur in Ishmael's story (21:9-21) and is used typologically by Paul in Galatians 4:24-25 to represent the covenant of Sinai.

---

### On 16:2 — אִוָּלֵד מִמֶּנָּה: The ibaneh / ben Wordplay (Key Crux)

**אִוָּלֵד מִמֶּנָּה** — "perhaps I shall be built up through her."

This is the **ibaneh / ben wordplay** noted as a key crux of the chapter. The Hebrew reads:

> אוּלַי אִבָּנֶה מִמֶּנָּה

The verb **אִבָּנֶה** is the Niphal imperfect 1cs of **בָּנָה** (banah) — "to build." The Niphal means "to be built" or, idiomatically, "to be built up, to have one's house/family established." But the form **אִבָּנֶה** also contains the word **בֵּן** (ben, "son") embedded within it — the listener/reader of Hebrew immediately hears **ben** inside **ibaneh**. The wordplay is intentional and theologically layered:

- **אִבָּנֶה** (ibaneh) = "I shall be built up" — the idea that a woman establishes her "house" (household/lineage) through bearing children; a barren woman is an "unbuilt" house.
- **בֵּן** (ben) = "son" — the mechanism of being built up is through a son.

The idiomatic phrase **בָּנָה בֵּית** ("to build a house" in the sense of establishing a family/lineage) is pervasive in the Hebrew Bible (Deut. 25:9; Ruth 4:11; 2 Sam. 7:11-13; 1 Kings 2:24). The LXX renders **אִבָּנֶה** as **οἰκοδομηθήσομαι** ("I shall be built up / I shall build a household") from **οἰκοδομέω** — acknowledging the building-household idiom but necessarily losing the embedded son-wordplay that Hebrew preserves.

The EMET renders "perhaps I shall be built up through her" to preserve the building idiom. The embedded **ben** can only be noted, not reproduced in English.

**עֲצָרַנִי יְהוָה מִלֶּדֶת** — "the LORD has restrained me from bearing."

Sarai's theological framing is striking: she does not say "I am infertile" or "it has not happened" — she attributes her childlessness directly to a divine action (**עָצַר**, atzar, "to restrain, shut up, hold back"). This is the frank speech of a woman who believes in divine sovereignty over the womb. The same verb is used of YHWH "closing every womb" in Gen. 20:18. Sarai's theology is not doubt but attribution: the LORD has done this, therefore Sarai herself devises the workaround. The plan she proposes is entirely within the legal custom of the ancient Near East (Nuzi texts, Old Babylonian law codes confirm the practice of a barren wife providing a slave-surrogate whose children are then legally counted as the wife's), but Sarai frames it with theological realism rather than despair.

---

### On 16:3 — לְאַבְרָם אִישָׁהּ לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה: Legal Formulation of the Arrangement

**וַתִּתֵּן אֹתָהּ לְאַבְרָם אִישָׁהּ לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה** — "and she gave her to Abram her husband as a wife for him."

The phrase **לְאִשָּׁה** ("as a wife") is the legal formula for taking a woman in a formal union — the same formula appears in 12:19 (Pharaoh "took her as a wife"), 25:1, and throughout Genesis for formal marital arrangements. Hagar is not merely a concubine in an informal sense; she is formally elevated to **אִשָּׁה** (ishah, "wife") — though with secondary status, as v. 4 makes clear she remains Sarai's **שִׁפְחָה**. The narrative precision matters: what Sarai gives Abram is not a casual arrangement but a legal one, which is precisely why the subsequent conflict has such weight — rights and status are genuinely at stake.

**מִקֵּץ עֶשֶׂר שָׁנִים לְשֶׁבֶת אַבְרָם בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן** — "at the end of ten years of Abram's dwelling in the land of Canaan."

The chronological notation places the Hagar episode exactly ten years after Abram's entry into Canaan (12:4-5). Abram was 75 at entry; he is now 85. Ishmael will be born at 86 (v. 16), and Isaac at 100 (21:5) — fourteen years after Ishmael. The precision of the chronology is part of Genesis's theological pattern of recording the long wait before promise-fulfillment: ten years of waiting, and now Sarai's solution.

---

### On 16:4 — וַתֵּקַל גְּבִרְתָּהּ בְּעֵינֶיהָ: Her Mistress Became Slight

**וַתֵּקַל גְּבִרְתָּהּ בְּעֵינֶיהָ** — "and her mistress became slight in her eyes."

The verb **קָלַל** (Qal perfect + Waw-consecutive here as Wayyiqtol) means "to be light, trifling, contemptible, of little account." The idiom **קָלַל בְּעֵינֵי** — "to be light/trivial in the eyes of" someone — means to become despised or regarded with contempt. Hagar's elevation from barren-mistress-serving-slave to pregnant-wife-bearing-heir triggers a reversal of social power: the formerly inferior party now regards the formerly superior party as less significant. This social dynamic — the fertile slave-wife looking down on the barren legal wife — is the precise situation the Nuzi and Old Babylonian legal texts sought to address: the law forbade the slave-wife from treating the legal wife as an equal once she had borne children. Hagar has violated the expected social code.

**גְּבִרְתָּהּ** (gevirtah) — "her mistress": the noun **גְּבֶרֶת** (geveret) is the feminine of **גֶּבֶר** (gever, "man, master") — "mistress, lady." It is a word of social power and authority. Sarai is Hagar's **גְּבֶרֶת** — the one above her. Hagar's contempt inverts this hierarchy. The word **גְּבִרְתָּהּ** will echo through vv. 8 and 9 as the angel twice speaks of "your mistress" — reaffirming the hierarchy Hagar has subverted.

---

### On 16:5 — חֲמָסִי עָלֶיךָ: Sarai's Accusation

**חֲמָסִי עָלֶיךָ** — "My wrong be upon you."

The noun **חָמָס** (chamas) is a powerful word: it means "violence, wrong, injustice, outrage" — the same word used in Gen. 6:11, 13 for the **חָמָס** that filled the earth before the Flood, and in Hab. 1:2-3 for the violence that cries out to God. Sarai's accusation is not a mild complaint but a formal legal declaration: "The outrage/injustice done to me is your fault." She is placing the moral weight of what has happened on Abram's shoulders: he who should have managed the household correctly has allowed Hagar to despise her.

The phrase **חֲמָסִי עָלֶיךָ** is a juristic formula — "my wrong [be] upon you" — a declaration of liability. Sarai is saying: "You are answerable for the injustice I am suffering." The LXX renders this **ἀδικοῦμαι ἐκ σοῦ** — "I am being wronged from/by you" — translating **חָמָס** as **ἀδικία** (injustice/wrong) and reading **עָלֶיךָ** as "from you/because of you." The LXX makes the accusation more personal and direct.

**יִשְׁפֹּט יְהוָה בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶיךָ** — "may the LORD judge between me and between you."

An appeal to divine arbitration — a judicial formula. Sarai cannot resolve the dispute by herself; she appeals to YHWH as the ultimate judge between the parties. The phrase **שָׁפַט בֵּין** ("to judge between") is the language of the divine judge (Gen. 31:53; 1 Sam. 24:13; Ezek. 34:17, 20). Ironically, Sarai has just attributed her own barrenness to YHWH's restraining (v. 2); now she appeals to YHWH to vindicate her. She is operating within a thoroughly theocentric framework even in her most human moments of anger.

---

### On 16:6 — וַתְּעַנֶּהָ שָׂרַי: Sarai's Affliction of Hagar

**וַתְּעַנֶּהָ שָׂרַי** — "and Sarai afflicted her."

The verb **עָנָה** (Piel) means "to afflict, oppress, humble, treat harshly." This is the same verb used for Israel's affliction in Egypt (Exod. 1:11, 12: "but the more they afflicted them [יְעַנּוּ אֹתוֹ]"; Deut. 26:6: "and the Egyptians afflicted us [וַיְעַנּוּנוּ]"). The lexical echo is striking and has been noted across centuries of Jewish and Christian interpretation: Hagar, an Egyptian, flees affliction at the hand of Sarai just as Israel will later flee affliction at the hand of Egypt. The roles of oppressor and oppressed cross ethnic and narrative lines.

The Piel verb is intense — this is not merely "she treated her harshly" in a mild sense but active, sustained oppression or humiliation. Abram's response — "your slave is in your hand, do what is good in your eyes" — is a complete abdication of protection for the woman he has just legally married. The narrative does not endorse this abdication; it presents it starkly. Hagar is alone.

---

### On 16:7 — מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה: The First Angel of the LORD in the Bible (Major Crux)

**וַיִּמְצָאָהּ מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה** — "And the angel of the LORD found her."

This is the **first appearance of the mal'akh YHWH** — the angel of the LORD — in the entire Bible. The significance of this fact has been recognized throughout the history of interpretation. Before this verse, neither the noun **מַלְאָךְ** (mal'akh, "messenger/angel") nor the phrase **מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה** ("messenger/angel of the LORD") has appeared in Genesis or anywhere in the Torah. Its inaugural appearance is addressed not to a patriarch but to **a runaway Egyptian slave-woman** — the most marginal of persons in the narrative world of Genesis.

**מַלְאַךְ** (mal'akh) — "messenger, angel": the word originally simply means "messenger" and refers to any sent one, divine or human. The construct phrase **מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה** ("messenger of YHWH") designates a specific divine agent whose identity in relation to YHWH is theologically complex and has been debated since antiquity:

1. **As a distinct divine being:** A created angelic messenger sent by YHWH, distinct from YHWH himself, who carries the divine name and authority but is not YHWH.
2. **As an appearance of YHWH himself (the identification view):** In many biblical texts, the **מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה** speaks in the first person as YHWH (cf. Judg. 2:1-3; 6:11-24; 13:3-22), is called YHWH by the human recipient, and appears to be identified with YHWH rather than simply speaking for him. Hagar herself names the **one who spoke to her** as YHWH directly in v. 13: **וַתִּקְרָא שֵׁם-יְהוָה הַדֹּבֵר אֵלֶיהָ** — "she called the name of the LORD who was speaking to her." This verse does not name the **מַלְאַךְ** but the **LORD** as her interlocutor. The blurring of distinction between the angel and YHWH is a persistent feature of mal'akh YHWH texts.
3. **Early Christian interpretation** (Justin Martyr, Origen, Tertullian) identified the **מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה** as a pre-incarnate appearance of the second person of the Trinity — the divine Son who becomes visible and speaks as YHWH while remaining distinct from the Father. This interpretation takes the identification view seriously while providing a theological framework for it.

The EMET translates **מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה** throughout as "the angel of the LORD" — the standard English rendering — while noting that this phrase carries a range of meaning from "divine messenger distinct from YHWH" to "theophanic manifestation of YHWH himself."

**עַל-עֵין הַמַּיִם בַּמִּדְבָּר עַל-הָעַיִן בְּדֶרֶךְ שׁוּר** — "at the spring of water in the wilderness — at the spring on the road to Shur."

The double identification of location is characteristic of narrative precision. **שׁוּר** (Shur) is a wilderness region east of Egypt, on the road back toward Hagar's homeland. She is fleeing toward Egypt. The spring becomes significant enough to be named (v. 14: Beer-lahai-roi), marking it as a place of theophany. The detail that she is on the road **to Shur** — the border of Egypt — tells us the direction of her flight: back toward the land she came from, seeking the only home she has ever known.

---

### On 16:8 — The Angel's Double Question

**מֵאַיִן בָּאת וְאָנָה תֵלֵכִי** — "from where have you come and where are you going?"

The two questions bracket Hagar's entire situation: origin and destination. They are not requests for information — the divine messenger knows the answers — but invitations to self-articulation. Hagar must name where she has come from (affliction at Sarai's hands) and face where she is going (back toward Egypt, away from the covenant household). Her answer addresses only the first question: "From the face of Sarai my mistress I am fleeing." She does not answer where she is going — perhaps because she does not know, or because the going-toward is less real to her than the fleeing-from. The angel's response (vv. 9-12) reorients her: she will go back, not forward.

**שִׁפְחַת שָׂרַי** — "slave of Sarai."

The angel addresses Hagar by her defining social relationship: she is Sarai's slave. This is not a reduction of Hagar's personhood but a naming of her situation that the divine messenger will now speak into. Three times in vv. 9-11 the angel speaks to her directly; she is seen, named, addressed, and given a divine promise. The contrast with Abram's silence about her (v. 6) is total.

---

### On 16:9 — וְהִתְעַנִּי תַּחַת יָדֶיהָ: Return and Submit

**שׁוּבִי אֶל-גְּבִרְתֵּךְ וְהִתְעַנִּי תַּחַת יָדֶיהָ** — "Return to your mistress and afflict yourself under her hands."

The divine command to Hagar is strikingly counter-intuitive: return to the woman who afflicted you and submit yourself under her authority. The verb **הִתְעַנִּי** (Hitpael of **עָנָה**) is the reflexive: "afflict yourself," i.e., submit to affliction, humble yourself. The Hitpael makes Hagar the subject of the affliction — not a passive victim but an active participant in a chosen submission.

This is not a divine endorsement of Sarai's abuse. The divine messenger does not say Sarai was right. What the divine command provides is a path forward: the promise of vv. 10-12 requires Hagar to return to the covenant household for the birth and raising of Ishmael. The promise cannot be received outside the context of the community. Moreover, the command comes with the most extraordinary divine promise: Hagar will have a seed too numerous to count (v. 10) — a promise structurally parallel to the promises given to Abram (15:5; 13:16). Hagar is told to return *not to servitude for its own sake* but because her future and her son's future are rooted in the household of the covenant.

---

### On 16:10 — הַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה אֶת-זַרְעֵךְ: The Parallel Blessing

**הַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה אֶת-זַרְעֵךְ** — "Multiplying I will multiply your seed."

The **infinitive absolute + Hiphil imperfect** of **רָבָה** (ravah, "to be many/multiply") — **הַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה** — is the same grammatical intensifier used elsewhere for the most emphatic divine promises: "I will indeed/certainly multiply." This is the seed-multiplication promise. Its structure is formally parallel to the promises given to Abram (12:2: "I will make you a great nation"; 13:16: "your seed like the dust of the earth"; 15:5: "so shall your seed be"). Hagar receives what Abram received: a divine promise of a seed too numerous to count.

**וְלֹא יִסָּפֵר מֵרֹב** — "and it will not be counted for multitude."

The phrase echoes 15:5 ("count the stars if you are able to count them") and 13:16 ("who can count the dust of the earth"). The uncountability of the promised seed is a formula of abundance and permanence. For the first time in the narrative, this formula is extended beyond Abram's direct lineage — Ishmael's descendants will also be innumerable.

---

### On 16:11 — יִשְׁמָעֵאל: The Name and Its Meaning (Key Crux)

**וְקָרָאת שְׁמוֹ יִשְׁמָעֵאל כִּי-שָׁמַע יְהוָה אֶל-עָנְיֵךְ** — "and you shall call his name Ishmael — for the LORD has heard your affliction."

The name **יִשְׁמָעֵאל** (Yishmael) is a sentence-name: **יִשְׁמַע** (yishma', "he/God hears") + **אֵל** (El, "God") = "God hears" or "God will hear." The etymology is made explicit by the angel: **כִּי-שָׁמַע יְהוָה אֶל-עָנְיֵךְ** — "for YHWH has heard your affliction/misery."

Three elements of this naming are theologically significant:

1. **The name is given by a divine messenger before birth** — one of only three such divine ante-natal namings in the entire Hebrew Bible (the others: Isaac, 17:19; and the servant of Deutero-Isaiah by inference). Ishmael's life is defined by his name before he lives it: he is "God-hears" because God heard his mother's affliction.

2. **עָנְיֵךְ (onyek, "your affliction"):** The root **עָנָה** that was used for Sarai's affliction of Hagar (v. 6: **וַתְּעַנֶּהָ**) reappears here. The angel names the very thing Sarai did to her as the ground for YHWH's action: "YHWH has heard your affliction." What was done to Hagar has been heard by God. The divine hearing is the counter-weight to the human oppression.

3. **YHWH, not El:** The naming formula uses **יְהוָה** (YHWH) even though the name Ishmael contains **אֵל** (El, "God"). The angel says "YHWH has heard" while the name enshrines "El hears." This is consistent with the biblical identification of YHWH as El — YHWH is the El who hears.

The **annunciation formula** here — "look, you are pregnant, and you will bear a son, and you shall call his name..." — is the precise pattern that will recur in the annunciations of Isaac (17:19), Samson (Judg. 13:3-5), and the Servant figure (Isa. 7:14; cf. Matt. 1:21; Luke 1:31). Hagar is the first woman in the Bible to receive a divine birth-annunciation, preceding Sarah by the entirety of Genesis 16 and 17.

---

### On 16:12 — פֶּרֶא אָדָם: The Wild-Donkey of a Man (Key Crux)

**וְהוּא יִהְיֶה פֶּרֶא אָדָם** — "And he will be a wild-donkey of a man."

**פֶּרֶא** (pere') is the wild onager — the wild donkey or wild ass of the ancient Near East (*Equus hemionus*), a notoriously untameable, fleet, and independent desert animal. The animal is used in the Hebrew Bible as a symbol of desert freedom and fierce independence: Job 39:5-8 provides the most sustained description: "Who has let the wild donkey go free? Who has loosed the bonds of the swift donkey, to whom I have given the arid plain for his home and the salt land for his dwelling place? He scorns the tumult of the city; he hears not the shouts of the driver. He ranges the mountains as his pasture and searches after every green thing." The comparison is not derogatory in the modern sense but evokes a primal, unconquerable independence — the kind of creature that cannot be domesticated or dominated.

**פֶּרֶא אָדָם** (pere' adam) — "wild-donkey of a man": a construct chain treating **אָדָם** as a genitive of species — "a man who is like/equivalent to the wild donkey." Not "a man who is a wild donkey" but "a wild-donkey-like man" — a man of the same fierce, untamed character. The LXX renders **ἄγροικος ἄνθρωπος** ("a rustic man," "a man of the fields/wilderness") — capturing the wild, outdoor character but losing the specific animal comparison. Jerome's Vulgate renders **homo agrestis** ("a wild/field man") similarly. The EMET preserves the literal "wild-donkey of a man."

**יָדוֹ בַכֹּל וְיַד כֹּל בּוֹ** — "his hand against everyone and everyone's hand against him."

This is a chiastic description of Ishmael's relationship with the world: **hand-his / against all** — **hand of all / against him**. The chiasm perfectly captures the reciprocal, perpetual conflict. It is not a curse but a description of a way of being — fierce, independent, combative, alive. The same dynamic is reflected in the historical traditions of Arabian tribal culture, which traced itself to Ishmael.

**וְעַל-פְּנֵי כָל-אֶחָיו יִשְׁכֹּן** — "and before the face of all his brothers he will dwell."

The phrase **עַל-פְּנֵי** literally means "upon the face of" — a preposition of spatial relationship: in front of, to the east of, in the presence of. The EMET renders "before the face of" to preserve the spatial literalism. The statement "before the face of all his brothers he will dwell" has been interpreted variously:

- **As confrontation:** He will dwell *against* all his brothers — in hostile proximity. This fits the "hand against everyone" framing.
- **As presence and persistence:** He will dwell *in the sight of* / *in the presence of* all his brothers — he will survive and continue despite the conflict. His dwelling is not hidden or marginal but visible.
- **As eastern dwelling:** **עַל-פְּנֵי** often means "east of" in geographic usage (4:16: Cain dwells **מִקֶּדֶם** east of Eden; but see 25:18: Ishmael's descendants dwell **עַל-פְּנֵי** all his brothers). The geographical sense may be that Ishmael's people will occupy the lands to the east and south of Canaan — the Arabian Peninsula — "before the face of" (i.e., to the east of) all their brother-nations. 25:18 confirms this: "And they settled from Havilah to Shur, which is before Egypt as you go toward Assyria. He settled before the face of all his brothers."

The EMET preserves the Hebrew spatial preposition and does not flatten the ambiguity.

---

### On 16:13 — אַתָּה אֵל רֳאִי: El Roi — Hagar Names God (Key Crux)

**וַתִּקְרָא שֵׁם-יְהוָה הַדֹּבֵר אֵלֶיהָ אַתָּה אֵל רֳאִי** — "And she called the name of the LORD who was speaking to her: You are El Roi."

This is one of the most remarkable moments in the entire book of Genesis. **Hagar names God.** This is the only place in the entire Bible where a human being — man or woman — gives a new name to God. Abram named Moriah in 22:14, but that is a place-name. Here, Hagar gives God a personal designation: **אֵל רֳאִי** (El Roi).

**אֵל רֳאִי** — the meaning is disputed and the vowelization is significant. The MT vowels **רֳאִי** with a hateph-qamets (a reduced-vowel a), giving a form that could be:
- **רֳאִי** from **רָאָה** (ra'ah, "to see"): "El of seeing" or "God who sees" — a God characterized by seeing, a God of vision.
- The construct form **רֳאִי** = "seeing [of me]" — "God who sees me" — the God who is my seer.
- Some read **רֹאִי** (ro'i) as a Qal active participle: "El the One-who-sees" = "the Seeing God."

The context makes the meaning clear even if the form is ambiguous: Hagar has been seen by God. The slave-woman fleeing in the wilderness, invisible to the social world, has been found and addressed by the divine messenger. She responds by naming God as the one who sees — because she has experienced being seen.

**כִּי אָמְרָה הֲגַם הֲלֹם רָאִיתִי אַחֲרֵי רֹאִי** — "for she said: Have I really seen here after the one who sees me?"

This verse is notoriously difficult. The EMET renders it with maximal literalism:

> **הֲגַם הֲלֹם רָאִיתִי** — "Have I really seen here" — **הֲגַם** (ha-gam) = "have I even/also/really?"; **הֲלֹם** = "here, in this place"; **רָאִיתִי** = "I have seen."

> **אַחֲרֵי רֹאִי** — "after the one who sees me" — **אַחֲרֵי** = "after, following"; **רֹאִי** = either "my seeing" (construct of action) or "the one who sees me" (participle + suffix).

The sentence seems to be Hagar's wondering exclamation of astonishment: "Have I really *here* seen [God] after [or following / in the wake of] the one who sees me?" In other words: "I have *seen* the one who *sees* me — and I am still alive." The mutuality of seeing — God sees Hagar, Hagar sees God — is the theological content of the moment.

The **play on the root רָאָה** (ra'ah, "to see") runs throughout vv. 13-14: **אֵל רֳאִי** (El Roi = God of seeing), **רָאִיתִי** (I have seen), **רֹאִי** (the one who sees me), **לַחַי רֹאִי** (Beer-lahai-roi = the well of the Living One who sees me). The verb of seeing is repeated five times in two verses.

Some ancient versions (LXX, Peshitta) translate the difficult rhetorical question with smoother sense but at the cost of the precise Hebrew. The LXX renders v. 13b: **καὶ εἶπεν· καὶ γὰρ ἐνώπιον εἶδον ὀφθέντα μοι** — "And she said: For indeed I have seen him who appeared to me face to face" — a paraphrase that loses the interrogative and the wordplay.

**This moment — a marginalized slave-woman naming God, in the wilderness, after experiencing divine sight — is one of the most theologically charged scenes in Genesis.** Hagar is the first person in the Bible to name God. She names him from her experience of being seen. The name she gives — *El Roi*, the God who sees — captures something essential about the divine character that no previous name in Genesis has expressed in quite this way.

---

### On 16:14 — בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי: Beer-lahai-roi (Key Crux)

**בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי** — "Beer-lahai-roi."

The well-name is a compound:
- **בְּאֵר** (be'er) = "well"
- **לַחַי** (la-chai) = "of the Living One" — **לְ** (to/of) + **חַי** (chai, "living, alive") — the Living One = YHWH, the God who is alive and the source of life
- **רֹאִי** (ro'i) = "who sees me" or "of my seeing" — the One who sees me / of seeing me

The full name: **"the well of the Living One who sees me"** — or more compactly: "the well of the Living-Seeing-One."

The name is a theological monument embedded in geography: this is the place where the Living God saw a runaway slave and spoke to her and promised her a future. The well itself becomes a marker of divine-human encounter, named for what happened there. It is mentioned again in 24:62 and 25:11 as a landmark in the Negev, associated with Isaac's territory — the half-brother of the son born at this place.

The name carries three affirmations: God is **living** (not a dead idol), God **sees** (not indifferent or absent), and the seeing is directed at **me** (personal, not merely cosmic). Hagar's naming of both God and the well constitute a two-stage theological act: first she names the divine (El Roi), then she names the place (Beer-lahai-roi). The place-name preserves the divine-name forever in the landscape.

**בֵּין-קָדֵשׁ וּבֵין בָּרֶד** — "between Kadesh and between Bered."

**קָדֵשׁ** (Kadesh) = the well-known wilderness site (also called Kadesh-barnea) on the southern border of Canaan in the Negev-Sinai region — a significant location in Israel's later wilderness wanderings (Num. 13:26; 20:1; Deut. 1:46). **בָּרֶד** (Bared) appears only here and its location is uncertain; it may be identified with a site in the Negev. The geographical note places Beer-lahai-roi firmly in the southern wilderness between Canaan and Egypt — the liminal zone between Sarai's world and Hagar's homeland.

---

### On 16:15-16 — Ishmael Born and Abram's Age

**וַתֵּלֶד הָגָר לְאַבְרָם בֵּן** — "And Hagar bore to Abram a son."

The birth notice is spare and formal. Hagar obediently returned (the narrative implies, though vv. 9-14 end before the return is narrated explicitly — the return is presupposed by v. 15). The birth is accomplished. The divine promise of v. 11 is immediately confirmed by the narrator.

**וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָם שֶׁם-בְּנוֹ אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה הָגָר יִשְׁמָעֵאל** — "And Abram called the name of his son whom Hagar bore Ishmael."

Abram performs the naming. This is notable: the angel commanded Hagar to name the child (v. 11: "you shall call his name Ishmael"), but Abram performs the naming. This is not necessarily a contradiction — both could participate in the naming, with Abram's act reflecting his acknowledgment of the child as his legitimate son. Abram names his son Ishmael, the name the angel gave, confirming that the divine annunciation reached him (presumably Hagar reported it to him upon her return).

**בֶּן-שְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה וְשֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים** — "the son of eighty-six years."

The Hebrew idiom for age is **בֶּן-X שָׁנָה** ("son of X years") — "Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael." The construction **שְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה וְשֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים** — "eighty years and six years" — gives the two components separately, a common biblical way of stating compound numbers. Abram will be 100 at Isaac's birth (21:5), so fourteen years separate Ishmael from Isaac. Ishmael is thirteen at the circumcision of Genesis 17 (17:25). These chronological markers are precise and internally consistent.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 16

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Codex Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 16:1 | שִׁפְחָה מִצְרִית | παιδίσκη Αἰγυπτία | LXX: παιδίσκη ("young female slave/girl") for **שִׁפְחָה**. Slightly softens the strict slave-status. Name: Ἁγάρ |
| 16:2 | אִוָּלֵד מִמֶּנָּה | οἰκοδομηθήσομαι ἐξ αὐτῆς | LXX: "I shall be built up from her" — confirms the building-up idiom and loses the embedded **ben**-wordplay, as expected from Greek |
| 16:2 | עֲצָרַנִי יְהוָה מִלֶּדֶת | συνέκλεισέν με κύριος τοῦ μὴ τίκτειν | LXX: "the LORD has shut me up from bearing" — συγκλείω = to shut up/enclose. Same force as MT **עָצַר** |
| 16:4 | וַתֵּקַל גְּבִרְתָּהּ בְּעֵינֶיהָ | ἠτιμάσθη ἡ κυρία αὐτῆς ἐναντίον αὐτῆς | LXX: "her mistress was dishonored before her" — ἀτιμάζω (to dishonor) for **קָלַל** (to be light/contemptible). Similar semantic range |
| 16:5 | חֲמָסִי עָלֶיךָ | ἀδικοῦμαι ἐκ σοῦ | LXX: "I am being wronged by you." Translates as active ongoing wrong; MT is a declaratory formula "my wrong be upon you" |
| 16:7 | מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה | ἄγγελος κυρίου | LXX: ἄγγελος κυρίου — exact equivalence, the standard LXX rendering throughout. First occurrence in both MT and LXX |
| 16:11 | כִּי-שָׁמַע יְהוָה אֶל-עָנְיֵךְ | ἐπήκουσεν κύριος τῇ ταπεινώσει σου | LXX: "the LORD heard your humiliation" — ταπείνωσις (humiliation/lowliness) for **עֳנִי** (affliction). Same root as the verb used for Sarai's afflicting of Hagar |
| 16:12 | פֶּרֶא אָדָם | ἄγροικος ἄνθρωπος | LXX: "a rustic/wild man" — loses the specific **pere'** (wild donkey) animal-comparison. Greek ἄγροικος means "of the fields, uncultured, rough" |
| 16:12 | וְעַל-פְּנֵי כָל-אֶחָיו יִשְׁכֹּן | κατὰ πρόσωπον πάντων τῶν ἀδελφῶν αὐτοῦ κατοικήσει | LXX: "he will dwell before the face of all his brothers" — exact equivalence with MT. κατὰ πρόσωπον = before the face |
| 16:13 | אַתָּה אֵל רֳאִי | σύ ὁ θεὸς ὁ ἐπιδών με | LXX: "you are the God who looked upon me" — ἐπιδών (aorist participle of ἐπιοράω, "to look upon"). Renders **רֳאִי** as past action "who looked upon me." Loses the wordplay and the etymological play on the name El Roi |
| 16:13 | הֲגַם הֲלֹם רָאִיתִי אַחֲרֵי רֹאִי | καὶ γὰρ ἐνώπιον εἶδον ὀφθέντα μοι | LXX: "for indeed I have seen the one who appeared to me face to face" — a smooth paraphrase losing the interrogative and the repetition of the seeing root |
| 16:14 | בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי | Φρέαρ οὗ ἐνώπιόν μου ὤφθη | LXX: "the well before which he appeared to me" — does not transliterate Beer-lahai-roi but explains it. Loses the **חַי** ("living") component entirely |

---

## Dead Sea Scrolls and Versional Notes

**Dead Sea Scrolls:** No significant Genesis 16 fragments are extant from Qumran. The chapter falls in a gap in the preserved scrolls. The Samaritan Pentateuch agrees with the MT throughout Genesis 16 without substantive variants; minor orthographic differences only.

**Targum Onkelos on 16:12:** Onkelos renders **פֶּרֶא אָדָם** as *gavar pereh* — "a wild man" (Aramaic) — following the same interpretive line as the LXX in capturing the wild-character without specifying the onager-animal. Onkelos renders **וְעַל-פְּנֵי כָל-אֶחָיו יִשְׁכֹּן** closely as "before all his brothers he will dwell" — affirming the geographic-relational reading.

**Targum Pseudo-Jonathan on 16:5:** Expands Sarai's complaint substantially, adding interpretive detail about the nature of Hagar's contempt and Abram's response. These additions reflect later rabbinic elaboration; the EMET follows the MT base text.

**Josephus (Antiquities I.10.4-5):** Josephus summarizes the Hagar narrative, identifying Ishmael as the ancestor of the Arab peoples and noting that the name means "heard by God." He preserves the essentials of the narrative without the theological depth of the biblical text.

**Paul's use of Hagar (Galatians 4:21-31):** Paul employs the Hagar-Sarah narrative as an allegory. Hagar represents the covenant of Sinai, the earthly Jerusalem, and slavery; Sarah represents the covenant of promise, the Jerusalem above, and freedom. Paul identifies the child of the slave-woman (Ishmael) with those who seek righteousness through the law and the child of the free woman (Isaac) with those who receive it by promise. This is an allegorical reading (*ἅτινά ἐστιν ἀλληγορούμενα*, Gal. 4:24) that Paul himself acknowledges goes beyond the plain narrative meaning. The EMET notes Paul's reading as a major trajectory of interpretation without adopting it as the primary meaning of the text. The primary meaning of Genesis 16 concerns Hagar, Ishmael, and the God who sees — and that primary meaning stands on its own.

---
